title: System Report with SysCheck
requirements: An SD card or USB drive, FAT32 formatted
An internet connection on the Wii (optional, for uploading the report)
_note: SysCheck doesn't change anything on your console — it's read-only. It's mainly useful for diagnosing IOS/cIOS problems or for pasting into a forum/troubleshooting thread.

steps.title: Before you start
steps.body:
**SysCheck** lists every IOS and cIOS on your Wii along with your System Menu version — a quick way to see exactly what's installed when something's misbehaving or you're asking for help online.

First, I'll check your SD card or USB drive is FAT32.

steps.title: Put SysCheck on your storage device
steps.body: Graphite downloads **SysCheck ModMii Edition (SysCheckME)** and unpacks it into the `apps` folder on your SD card or USB drive.

steps.title: Clean up and eject the card
steps.body:
macOS scatters hidden junk files (`.DS_Store`, `._` files) onto SD cards and USB drives, and Wii homebrew can choke on them.

Press the button, and Graphite strips those files off the card and **safely ejects it for you**. Once it pops off, pull it out.

steps.title: Run SysCheck
steps.body:
Plug the storage device into your Wii and power it on. Launch the **Homebrew Channel** and open **SysCheck**.

1. Let it gather your console's initial data.
2. Press **A** when prompted to test each IOS.
3. Wait while it analyzes your IOS/cIOS configuration.
4. Press **A** to view the finished log.

When done, a `syscheck.csv` file is saved to the root of your storage device. If your Wii is online, you can also press **A** to upload it directly to `syscheck.rc24.xyz` for a shareable link.

steps.title: You're done
steps.body: You now have a full report of your Wii's IOS/cIOS setup, either as `syscheck.csv` on your storage device or as a link from `syscheck.rc24.xyz`. Share it in a forum or troubleshooting thread if you're trying to diagnose an IOS conflict.
